Facts.text: Maya city of Dos Pilas attacks and conquers the larger and older city of Seibal
St Zachary selected as Catholic Pope to succeed Gregory III [or Dec 5]
Pope Clement VI declares Roman tribunal Cola di Rienzo as heretics
1st Covenant of Scottish protestants form
Sir Thomas Herriot introduces potatoes to England from Colombia
1st annulment by court decree passes
Battle of Lund (Scanian War): Swedish army of 8,000 defeats much larger joint Danish/Dutch force of 13,000
Charles II bars Jews from settling in Stockholm, Sweden
First recorded successful separation of conjoined twins Elisabet and Catherina Meijerin, completed by Swiss surgeon Johannes Fatio in Basel
English parliamentary election set for every 3 years
Baron Jacob Hop appointed treasurer-general of the Hague
First official US flag raised (Grand Union Flag) aboard naval vessel USS Alfred
Illinois becomes 21st state USA (Admission day)
Oberlin College in Ohio, the first truly coeducational college, opens
First US dental society organized (NY)
First US mutual fire insurance company issues first policy (Rhode Island)
RC Society Apostole of Prayer forms
Irishman James Kelly (24) and British soldier Jack Smith (34) fight longest bareknuckle boxing bout - 6 hours and 15 minutes, at Fiery Creek, Victoria, Australia
Skirmish at Thomas' Station, Georgia

English Assyriologist George Smith reads his translation of The Great Flood from the "Epic of Gilgamesh" to the Society of Biblical Archaeology, with striking similarities to the later flood of Noah in "Genesis"
Settlers arrive at Petach Tikvah, Israel
48th US Congress (1883-85) convenes
At the MLB meeting, the Milwaukee Brewers franchise is officially dropped from the American League and replaced by the St. Louis Browns
The Sultanate of Aceh's Panglima Polim surrenders to Dutch Army Captain Hendrikus Colijn at Atjeh
Neon lights first publicly displayed (Paris Auto Show)
First Balkan War: The Naval Battle of Elli takes place.
Gerrit Brinkman becomes 1st Dutch traffic officer
Turkey, Serbia, Montenegro, Greece & Bulgaria sign weapons pact
Dutch army opens fire on interned Belgian soldiers: 8 killed
General Joseph Joffre becomes Commander-in-Chief of the French Armies
After nearly 20 years of planning and construction, the Quebec Bridge finally opens to traffic after two prior collapses
The Supreme Allied War Council, meeting at Versailles to define war aim, fails to reach an agreement
Turkey & Armenia agree to peace treaty
First Congressional open session broadcast via radio (Washington, D.C.)
Manchester Guardian (German Reichswehr/Red Army work together)
Airborn chemicals combine with fog to kill 60 (Meuse Valley, Belgium)
Otto Ender forms Austrian government
Alka Seltzer goes on sale
Gen Kurt von Schleicher becomes chancellor of Germany
Joe Lilliard QBs Chic Cardinals; last NFL black until 1946
Italian colonial Tripoli & Cyrenaica annexed to Libya
KYW-AM in Chicago Ill moves to Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
AAUs decides to continue linear measuring system over metric
9th Heisman Trophy Award: Angelo Bertelli, Notre Dame (QB)
World War II: Battle of Monte Cassino, Italy begins
Britain's Home Guard ('Dad's Army') is officially stood down at a special farewell parade in Hyde Park, London.
British order to disarm causes general strike in Greece
Hungarian death march of Jews ends
Mussert puts Seyss-Inquart plan for small Nazi-Europe
Temporary merger of 2 NFL teams, the Pittsburgh Steelers and Chicago Cardinals, dissolves at finish of season; WWII ends before start of 1945 season, so both teams resume normal operations
The Greek Civil War breaks out in a newly-liberated Greece, between communists and royalists
US 5th Armoured division occupies Brandenburg Hurtgenwald
12th Heisman Trophy Award: Glenn Davis, Army (HB)
"Pumpkin Papers" come to light (claimed to be from Alger Hiss)
1st US woman army officer not in medical corps sworn in
Chinese refugee ship "Kiangya" explodes in East China Sea killing 1,100
KRLD (now KDFW) TV channel 4 in Dallas-Fort Worth, Texas (CBS) begins broadcasting
Cleveland Browns last NFL team with no-pass game (beat Philadelphia 13-7)
1st TV broadcast in Hawaii
Marcos Perez Jiménez elected president of Venezuela
Webb Pierce records single "In the Jailhouse Now" (Billboard Song of the Year, 1955)
William Walton's opera "Troilus & Cressida" premieres in London
KTVE TV channel 10 in Monroe-El Dorado, LA (NBC) begins broadcasting

Australian 4 x 200m freestyle relay team of Kevin O'Halloran, John Devitt, Murray Rose & Jon Henricks swim world record 8:23.6 to take the gold medal at the Melbourne Olympics; Rose's 2nd of 3 gold at the Games
Britain and France pull troops out of Egypt
KFSA (now KFSM) TV channel 5 in Ft Smith, AR (CBS) 1st broadcast
23rd Heisman Trophy Award: John Crow, Texas A&M (HB)
Indonesian parliament accepts nationalisation of Dutch businesses
State of emergency in Cyprus ends
US performs underground nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
1963 NFL Draft: Terry Baker from Oregon State first pick by Los Angeles Rams
Edith Sampson sworn-in as 1st Black female judge in the state of Illinois
Pravda criticizes western art
KHQL (now KCAN) TV channel 8 in Albion, NB (ABC) begins broadcasting
Police arrests 800 sit-in students at University of California at Berkeley
"Rubber Soul", the sixth studio album by the Beatles, is released in the United Kingdom
The Beatles begin final UK concert tour in Glasgow, Scotland
USSR launches Luna 8; it crashes on the Moon
Television pop group "The Monkees" make their live concert debut at the Honolulu International Centre Arena, in Hawaii
US performs underground nuclear test at Hattiesburg, Mississippi
Australian Derek Clayton runs world marathon record 2:09:36.4 at the Fukuoka Marathon in Japan
Final run of "20th Century Limited" famed NY-Chicago luxury train

MLB Rules Committee adopts a series of changes designed to increase amount of offensive run production; decreases strike zone size and lowers height of pitcher's mound from 15" to 10"; changes are successful
Claude Ruel resigns as coach of the NHL Montreal Canadiens; replacement Al MacNeil leads Habs to a Stanley Cup championship 5 months later
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
India invades West Pakistan claiming hundreds of lives and starting the full scale Indo-Pakistani War of 1971
Convair 990A charter crashes in Tenerife Canary Island, 155 die
Pioneer 10 passes Jupiter (1st fly-by of an outer planet) [Dec 4-GMT]
Laos falls to communist forces; Lao People's Democratic Rep proclaimed
Patrick J Hillery elected President of Ireland
11 members of the audience trampled to death during a stampede to claim unreserved seats for a concert by The Who at The Riverfront Coliseum, Cincinnati, Ohio
45th Heisman Trophy Award: Charles White, Southern California (RB)
Christies auctions a thimble for a record $18,400
Iran accepts constitution
Shadow Traffic begins broadcasting in the New York City metropolitan area.
NY Federal jury finds Reps Thompson D-NJ & Murphy, D-NY, guilty
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
35.7 cm rainfall at Big Fork, Arkansas (state record)
77°F highest December temperature ever recorded in Cleveland, Ohio
49th Heisman Trophy Award: Mike Rozier, Nebraska (RB)
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll

Bhopal disaster: A Union Carbide pesticide plant leaks 45 tons of methyl isocyanate and other toxic compounds in Bhopal, India, officially killing 2,259 - other estimates are as high as 16,000 (including later deaths) and over half a million injured
23rd Shuttle Mission (61-B)-Atlantis 2-lands at Edwards AFB
54th Heisman Trophy Award: Barry Sanders, Oklahoma State (RB)
NY Lotto pays $45 million to twelve winner (#s are 1-8-13-18-28-48)
Muslim Shi'ites in Beirut, Lebanon release US political hostage Alann Steen
White House Chief of Staff John Sununu resigns
The Greek oil tanker Aegean Sea, carrying 80,000 tonnes of crude oil, runs aground in a storm while approaching La Coruña, Spain, and spills much of its cargo
UN Security Council passes Resolution 794 authorizing US led allied forces to intervene in war-torn Somalia to restore order and allow humanitarian aid to be delivered
England cricket wicket keeper Jack Russell dismisses Clive Eksteen with his record 11th catch in 2nd Test v South Africa in Johannesburg
Naeem Akhtar takes 10-28 for Rawalpindi B against Peshawar
Northwestern South Carolina begins using new area code 864
"1776" opens at Gershwin Theater NYC
Golden State Warriors terminates guard Latrell Sprewell's four-year, $32 million NBA contract for attacking coach P.J. Carlesimo
NASA loses radio contact with the Mars Polar Lander moments before the spacecraft enters the Martian atmosphere
Ottawa Senators' right wing Kevin Dineen becomes 5th player in NHL history to score 300 career goals and record 2,000 penalty minutes; picks up a misconduct penalty in 7-4 loss to NJ Devils
Worcester cold storage and warehouse fire in Worcester, Massachusetts claims lives of 6 firefighters
XCOR Aerospace makes first manned rocket aircraft delivery of US Mail in Mojave, California.

Devastating winter storms cause Chehalis River to flood many cities in Lewis County, Washington, closing 20-mile portion of Interstate 5 for several days, resulting in at least eight deaths and billions of dollars of damage
Australian Rugby star David Pocock is arrested after protesting against a coal mine under construction in an Australian national forest
Darren Wilson, the police officer who shot dead unarmed black teenager Michael Brown in Ferguson, Missouri, resigns
Protests erupt in cities across the US after a grand jury decides not to charge the New York City police officer who killed Eric Garner with a choke-hold
US Defense Secretary Ash Carter announces all combat roles in US armed forced will be opened to women
US army decides it will not allow an oil pipeline to be built in North Dakota, after months of protests by The Standing Rock Sioux Tribe
First pizza party in space held by astronauts of the International Space Station
7-year old Ryan of Ryan Toysreview is the year's highest-paid YouTube star earning $22 million
Dutch court in Arnhem rejects man's request to legally be recognised 20 years younger
AT&T Inc’s Warner Bros studio announces all its 2021 movies will stream online the same day they appear in theatres because of the pandemic
“I actually believe they’re going to be the most difficult time in the public health history of this nation,” warns Dr. Robert Redfield, head of US CDC about the months ahead with COVID-19
FBI investigate a targeted attack on two electricity substations in North Carolina which closes schools and leaves around 40,000 without power for days

Former US PresidentDonald Trumpcalls in a Truth Social post for the "termination" of all rules, including the US Constitution, to overturn the 2020 election, which he again falsely claims that he won.
Mosconi Cup nine-ball pool, Las Vegas, Nevada: two-time defending champions Team Europe dominate US, 11-7; MVP: Joshua Filler (Germany)
Indonesia's Mount Marapi, on island of Sumatra, erupts 3km into the air, killing 23 people
Israeli ground forces begin offensive in southern Gaza, north of Khan Younis after a seven-day ceasefire expires
Namibia elects its first woman president, vice-president Netumbo Nandi-Ndaitwah
Edmond Halleyreceives MA from The Queen's College, Oxford
Colley Cibberis appointed British Poet Laureate under KingGeorge II
AstronomerAnders Celsiustakes measurements that confirm Newton's theory that the earth was an ellipsoid rather than the previously accepted sphere
Andrew Jacksonelected 7th US President
Frederick Douglasspublishes first issue of his newspaper "North Star"
Confederate GeneralJames Longstreetabandons his siege at Knoxville, Tennessee
Lawyers for Confederate PresidentJefferson Davisargue before US judges Salmon Chase and John Underwood to have treason charges dismissed as unconstitutional double jeopardy
Henry Morton Stanleyfounds Leopoldville (now Kinshasa)
George M. Cohan's musical "Talk of the Town" premieres in NYC
Edward Elgar's 1st Symphony in A performed by the Hallé Orchestra conducted by Hans Richter, premieres in Manchester, England
Willis Carrierpresents his influential "Rational Psychrometric Formulae", on air conditioning to the American Society of Mechanical Engineers - makes air conditioning possible
Detective novelistAgatha Christiemysteriously disappears for 11 days
Boston Bruins beat the Montreal Canadiens 3-1, the first win of an NHL record 14-game winning streak, and a 22-game home winning streak
Richard RodgersandLorenz Hart's musical "Evergreen" premieres in London
As part of a famous MLB fire sale of players, Philadelphia A's ownerConnie Macksells catcherMickey Cochraneto Detroit for $100,000; Cochrane is immediately named Tigers manager
Funeral of WWI commander SirArthur Curriewith 150,000 people lining the streets of Montreal - largest Canadian funeral at the time
Adolf Hitlerviews Poltava, Ukraine
Howard Hansonconducts premiere of his 4th Symphony ("Requiem") with the Boston Symphony; wins Pulitzer Prize for Music, 1944
US government asks UN to order dictatorFrancisco Francoout of Spain

Tennessee Williams' stage drama "A Streetcar Named Desire", starringJessica Tandy,Marlon Brando, and Kin Hunter, and directed byElia Kazanopens the Ethel Barrymore Theatre, NYC; runs for 855 performances, wins a Tony Award and a Pulitzer Prize
Premiere ofDmitri Shostakovich' 5th String Quartet in Leningrad
US PresidentDwight Eisenhowercriticizes Wisconsin Senator Joe McCarthy for saying communists are in the Republican Party
Samuel Barber's cantata "Prayers of Kierkegaard" premieres
Basketball iconWilt Chamberlainmakes his much anticipated varsity debut; scores 52 points and grabs 31 rebounds, breaking both all-time Kansas records in an 87–69 win against Northwestern
DutchmanAnton Geesinkbecomes 1st non-Japanese judo world champion
George Blandaof Houston Oilers kicks 55-yard field goal
Assassination attempt made onBob Marleyand others during concert rehearsals in Jamaica
First human heart transplant performed in South Africa by Dr.Christiaan Barnardon Louis Washkansky
Fomrer Indonesianpresident Sukarnoplaced under house arrest
Elvis Presley's Comeback Special airs on NBC, his 1st live performance in seven years, re-launching his singing career

Don Sutton, the winningest pitcher in Los Angeles Dodgers history, signs a 4-year contract with Houston; Astros career shortened by a patellar fracture; returns to Dodgers in 1988 after stints with 3 other MLB clubs
"Frances", film depicting life of actress Frances Farmer and starringJessica Lange, first released in the US
After losing his world welterweight boxing titles toSugar Ray Leonard,Thomas Hearnswins WBC Super Welterweight crown in a majority points decision over Wilfred Benitez at Superdome, New Orleans
"Do They Know It's Christmas" single written byBob Geldofand Midge Ure and sung by an all-star cast under the banner of Band Aid is released in the UK
Soviet PresidentMikhail Gorbachevand US PresidentGeorge H. W. Bush, declare the Cold War over
Stefan Edbergof Sweden claims his first and only ATP Masters Grand Prix tennis title with a 4–6, 7–6, 6–3, 6–1 win over defending championBoris Beckerat Madison Square Garden, NYC
French Open champion Gustavo Kuerten of Brazil wins the season-ending Tennis Masters Cup for the only time; beatsAndre Agassi6–4, 6–4, 6–4 in the final in Lisbon, Portugal
Gilberto Rodríguez Orejuelais extradited to the United States
Patrick Kanesigns a five-year, $31.5 million contract extension with the Chicago Blackhawks
Ballon d'Or: Real Madrid midfielderLuka Modrićwins; first time since 2007 that eitherLionel MessiorCristiano Ronaldodoesn't win award; Norwegian striker Ada Hegerberg is inaugural women's winner
David Attenboroughwarns collapse of civilisation and the natural world on the horizon at UN climate summit in Poland
70th anniversary of NATO marked by gathering in London of world leaders and reception byElizabeth IIat Buckingham Palace
Google co-foundersLarry PageandSergey Brinannounce they are stepping down from roles at parent company Alphabet,Sundar Pichaito become head of both
Kamala Harrisends her campaign to be the Democratic candidate for president
Spotify sayDrakethe most streamed star of the 2010s with 28 billion streams,Ed Sheeran's "Shape Of You" most streamed track with 2.4 billion streams
World leaders discussing US PresidentDonald Trumpin unflattering terms at NATO reception caught on camera and goes viral

Kennedy Center Honors ceremony held for pop singerDionne Warwick, singer-songwriterBarry Gibb, comic-actorBilly Crystal, operatic soprano Renée Fleming, and singer-actressQueen Latifah, in Washington D.C.
South Korean president PresidentYoon Suk Yeoldeclares martial law for the first time in 50 years, prompting an emergency vote against it by the National Assembly
Eureka Stockade: In what is claimed by many to be the birth of Australian democracy, more than 20 goldminers at Ballarat, Victoria, are killed by state troopers in an uprising over mining licences
Nicolaus von Amsdorf, German reformation theologist, born in Torgau, Saxony (d. 1565)
Kanaka Dasa, Indian musician and composer of Carnatic music, born in Baada, Vijayanagara Empire (now Karnataka, India) (d. 1609)
Jan Gruter, Flemish-born Dutch critic, born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1627)
Marsilio Casentini, Italian composer, born in Trieste, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1651)
Nicolo Amati, Italian violin maker (Stradivari & Guarneri), born in Cremona, Duchy of Milan, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1684)
Casimir Schweizelsperg, German composer, born in Rosenheim, Upper Bavaria (d. sometime after 1722)
Ludvig Holberg, Baron of Holberg, founder of modern Danish and Norwegian literature, born in Bergen, Denmark-Norway (d. 1754)
Hryhorii Skovoroda, Ukrainian poet and philosopher, born in Chernukhi, Lubny Regiment, Cossack Hetmanate (d. 1794)
Antonio Soler, Spanish Catalan composer (Fandango), born in Olot, Catalonia, Spain (d. 1783)
Johann Franz Xaver Sterkel, German organist, pianist, kapellmeister, composer, and priest, born in Würzburg,  Prince-Bishopric of Würzburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1817)
Georg Friederich Fuchs, German composer, born in Mainz, Landgraviate of Hesse (d. 1821)
Samuel Crompton, English inventor (mule-jenny spinning machine), born in 10 Firwood Fold, Bolton, Lancashire, England (d. 1827)
Gilbert Stuart, American portrait painter (unfinished portrait of George Washington), born in Saunderstown, Rhode Island (d. 1828)
Joseph Gelinek, Czech pianist and composer, born in Sedlec, KIngdom of Bohemia (d. 1825)
Mary Lamb, British author (Tales from Shakespeare), born in London, England (d. 1847)
Francis Kenrick, Irish-American Archbishop of Baltimore (1851-63), born in Dublin, Kingdom of Ireland (d. 1863)
France Prešeren, Slovenian poet (Krst pri Savici), born in Vrba, Carniola, Habsburg Monarchy (d. 1849)
Henry A. Wise, American lawyer and politician (33rd Governor of Virginia), born in Drummondtown, Virginia (d. 1876)
Thomas Alfred Davies, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Black Lake, New York (d. 1899)
Charles Adam Heckman, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Easton, Pennsylvania (d. 1896)
Edmond van der Straeten, Belgian lawyer and musicologist, born in Oudenaarde, Belgium (d. 1895)
Green Berry Raum, American lawyer, politician, author, and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Golconda, Illinois (d. 1909)
Cleveland Abbe, American meteorologist (Father of Weather Bureau), born in New York City (d. 1916)
Octavia Hill, British reformer, leader of open-space movement, born in London, England (d. 1912)
Charles Alfred Pillsbury, American industrialist and co-founder (Pillsbury Company), born in Warner, New Hampshire (d. 1899)
Ellen Henrietta Richards, American chemist (Amer Home Economics Association-1st Pres), born in Dunstable, Massachusetts (d. 1911)
Phoebe Hearst, American philanthropist and suffragist, born in Franklin County, Missouri (d. 1919)
William Milton, English cricket all-rounder (3 Tests South Africa, 2 wickets) and rugby union centre (2 Tests England), born in Little Marlow, England (d. 1930)
Jules Renkin, Belgian politician (28th Prime Minister of Belgium 1931-32), born in Ixelles, Belgium (d. 1934)
Herman Heijermans, Dutch writer (Kamertjeszonde, Diamond City), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1924)
Bernhard Lichtenberg, German clergyman and theologian, born in Ohlau, Prussian Silesia, Kingdom of Prussia, German Empire (d. 1943)
Max Meldrum, Scottish-Australian painter (founder of Australian Tonalism), born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1955)
Richard Pearse, New Zealand inventor and aviator who designed and flew one of the world's first airplanes, born at Waitohi Flat, New Zealand (d. 1953)
Albert Asher, New Zealand dual-code rugby union and rugby league winger (RU 11 caps; RL Australasia 2 Tests, NZ 1), born in Tauranga, New Zealand (d. 1965)

Fedor von Bock, German field marshal (commander in the German occupation of Austria, invasions of Poland, France, and Russia during WWII), born in Cüstrin, Province of Brandenburg, German Empire (d. 1945)
Nandalal Bose, Indian pioneering modern artist (illustrated Constitution of India), born in Haveli Kharagpur, British India (d. 1966)
Anton (Friedrich Wilhelm) von Webern, Austrian 12-tone composer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1945)

Tibby Cotter, Australian cricket fast bowler (21 Tests, 49 wickets) and solder (killed in world's last successful cavalry charge, 4th Light Horse Brigade at Beersheba), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1917)
Walther Stampfli, Swiss politician and member of the Swiss Federal Council, born in Büren, Solothurn, Switzerland (d. 1965)
Manne Siegbahn, Swedish physicist (Nobel 1924-discoveries and research in the field of X-ray spectroscopy), born in Örebro, Sweden (d. 1978)
Naruhiko Higashikuni, Japanese Imperial Prince and 30th Prime Minister of Japan for 54 days (1945), born in Kyoto, Japan (d. 1990)
Algernon Kingscote, English tennis player (Australasian C'ship 1919), born in Bangalore, India (d. 1964)
Ion Nonna Otescu, Romanian composer and educator (Bucharest Conservatory, 1913-40), born in Bucharest, Romania (d. 1940)
Julius Bissier, German painter, born in Freiburg im Breisgau, Germany (d. 1965)
Bert Baston, American College Football Hall of Fame end (University of Minnesota), born in St. Louis Park, Minnesota (d. 1979)
Anna Freud, Austrian-English psychoanalyst and daughter of Sigmund Freud, born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 1982)
Bolesław Szabelski, Polish organist and composer, born in Radoryż Smolany, Russian Poland (d. 1979)
John Urzidil, Austrian-American writer (Die erbeuteten Frauen), born in Prague (d. 1970)
Kate O'Brien, Irish writer (Without My Cloak), born in Limerick City, Ireland (d. 1974)
Lev Knipper, Russian-Soviet composer (Poem of the Komsomol Fighter; Tales of a Plaster God), and secret agent, born in Tiflis, Russian Empire (now Tbilisi, Georgia) (d. 1974)
Howard Kinsey, American tennis player (French Open doubles 1924, US Open doubles 1926; Wimbledon singles 1926 runner-up), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1966)
Ikeda Hayato, Prime Minister of Japan (1960-64), born in Takehara, Hiroshima, Japan (d. 1965)
Richard Kuhn, Austrian-German biochemist (Nobel 1938-work on carotenoids and vitamins), born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 1967)
Ulrich Inderbinen, Swiss mountain guide, born in Zermatt, Valais (d. 2004)
Glenn Hartranft, American athlete (discus WR 47.89m 1925; Olympic silver shot putt 1924), born in Aberdeen, South Dakota (d. 1970)
Edgar Moon, Australian tennis player (Australian C'ships 1930), born in Forest Hill NSW (d. 1976)
Les Ames, English cricket wicket-keeper/batsman (47 Tests, 97 dismissals, 2,434 runs @ 40.56; Kent CCC), born in Elham, England (d. 1990)
Andrew Hutchings, British trade union leader, born in England (d. 1996)
Connee Boswell, American jazz singer (The Boswell Sisters; Pete Kelly's Blues), born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 1976)
Machito [Francisco "Frank" Grillo], Latin jazz musician and creator of Cu-bop and salsa music (Afro-Cubans), born in Havana, Cuba - NB: birth date and location disputed (d. 1984)
Edward Underdown, British actor (Beat the Devil, Thunderball), born in London, England (d. 1989)
Halsey Stevens, American composer (Triskelion), born in Scott, New York (d. 1989)
Nigel Balchin, English author (Business for Pleasure), born in Potterne, England (d. 1970)
Dana Suesse, American musician and songwriter (You Ought To Be In Pictures), born in Kansas City, Jackson County, Missouri (d. 1987)
Robert Arthur Hughes, British missionary and surgeon who worked in India, born in Oswestry, England (d. 1996)
Nino Rota [Giovanni Rota Rinaldi], Italian classical and film score composer (Il cappello di paglia di Firenze; The Godfather (I & II); 16 Federico Fellini films), born in Milan, Italy (d. 1979)
Omer Vanaudenhoven, Flemish politician and resistance fighter, born in Diest, Belgium (d. 1994)
Edward "Corky" Cornelius, American jazz trumpeter (Benny Goodman; Gene Krupa), born in Cicero, Indiana (d. 1943)
Irving Fine, American composer (Toccata), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1962)
Abdul Haris Nasution, Indonesian army general, born in Koetanopan, North Sumatera, Dutch East Indies (d. 2000)
Charles Craig, English operatic tenor, born in London (d. 1997)
Hans G. Kresse, Dutch cartoonist (known for the comic strip Eric de Noorman), born in the Netherlands (d. 1992)
Phyllis Curtin, American soprano (NYC Opera, 1953-64), born in Clarksburg, West Virginia (d. 2016)
Joe Collins, American baseball first baseman (World Series 1949–53, 56; New York Yankees), born in Scranton, Pennsylvania (d. 1989)
Sven Nykvist, Swedish cinematographer (Cries and Whispers), born in Moheda, Kronobergs län, Sweden (d. 2006)

Tom Fears, American College and Pro Football Hall of Fame defensive end and coach (NFL champion 1951; First-team All-Pro, NFL receiving yards leader 1950; LA Rams), born in Guadalajara, Mexico (d. 2000)
Trevor Bailey, English cricket all-rounder, writer and broadcaster (61 Tests, 1 x 100, 10 x 50, HS 134no, 132 wickets; BBC), born in Westcliff-on-Sea, Essex, England (d. 2011)
F. Sionil José, Filipino novelist (Rosales Saga) and Philippine National Artist for Literature, born in Rosales, Pangasinan, Philippine Islands (d. 2022)
Fred Taylor, American Basketball Hall of Fame coach (Ohio State University 1959-76; MLB Washington Senators 1950-52), born in Zanesville, Ohio (d. 2002)
John Backus, American computer scientist and inventor (FORTRAN computer language), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2007)
Roberto Mières, Argentine auto racer (USAC Road Racing C'ship Daytona 1,000 km 1959; 17 x F1 GP), born in Mar del Plata, Argentina (d. 2012)
Wiel Coerver, Dutch soccer manager (developer of Coerver Method, football coaching technique), born in Kerkrade, Netherlands (d. 2011)
Ferlin Husky, American country singer (Born to Lose; Gone), born in Desloge, Missouri (d. 2011)
Ken Funston, South African cricket batsman (18 Tests, 5 x 50, HS 92; NE Transvaal, Transvaal, Orange Free State), born in Pretoria, Transvaal, South Africa (d. 2005)
Hans Otte, German composer and pianist (The Book of Sounds, Book of Hours), born in Plauen, Germany (d. 2007)
Walter Ormeño, Peruvian soccer goalkeeper (Peru 1949-57; Boca Juniors, Rosario Central), born in Lima, Peru (d. 2020)
Isabelle Harriet Lucas, Canadian-born British actress and singer (Outland, Comics), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 1997)
Jack McMahon, American basketball guard and coach (player Rochester Royal, St. Louis Hawks; coach Cincinnati Royals, San Diego Rockets, Pittsburgh Condors), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1989)
Clarence Ford, American jazz and R&B clarinet and saxophone player, born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 1994)
Paul Turok, American composer (Ragtime Caprice; Aspects of Lincoln and Liberty), educator, and music critic, born in New York City (d. 2012)
Werner Rackwitz, German opera director and musicologist (Handel-Renaissance), born in Breslau, Weimar Republic (d. 2014)
Ellen Blazer [Esther Clara Blazer], Dutch TV director (Sonja), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 2013)
Franz Josef Degenhardt, German poet and author, born in Schwelm, Westphalia, Germany (d. 2011)
Jaye P. Morgan, American pop singer ("That's All I Want From You", "The Longest Walk"), actress, and game show panelist (The Gong Show), born in Mancos, Colorado
Bob Cranshaw, American jazz bassist (Blue Note Records; Sonny Rollins; Stanley Turrentine; Duke Pearson), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2016)
Corry Brokken, Dutch pop singer (1957 Eurovision), born in Breda, The Netherlands (d. 2016)
Paul J. Crutzen, Dutch meteorologist and chemist, (1995 Nobel Prize for Chemistry for work on atmospheric chemistry ), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands
Rosalind Knight, British stage and screen actress (Friday Night Dinner - "Horrible Grandma"), born in Marylebone (now Westminster), England (d. 2020)
Georges Aillères, French rugby league forward & captain (34 Tests; Toulouse Olympique, FC Lézignan Sangliers), born in Poucharramet, Haute-Garonne, France (d. 2024)
Nicolas Coster, American character actor (Santa Barbara; One Life To Live; The Facts of Life), born in London, England (d. 2023)
Viktor Gorbatko, Soviet cosmonaut (Soyuz 7, 24, 37/36), born in Ventsy-Zarya in Krasnodar Krai, Soviet Union (d. 2017)

Mary Alice [Smith], American stage and screen actress (Fences - Tony Award, 1987; I'll Fly Away - Emmy Award, 1993; A Different World "Lettie"), born in Indianola, Mississippi (d. 2022) [some sources give birth year as 1941]
Bobby Boyd, American NFL cornerback (5 × All-Pro 1964–1968; NFL interceptions leader 1965; Baltimore Colts), born in Dallas, Texas (d. 2017)
Stephen Rubin, English businessman (Pentland Group - UK's largest sportwear company), born in England
José Serebrier, Uruguayan conductor and composer (Star Wagon; Passacaglia and Perpetuum Mobile), born in Montevideo, Uruguay
Jeffrey R. Holland, American religious leader on the Mormon church, born in St. George, Utah
Kishin Shinoyama, Japanese photographer, including album cover art (Saori Minami; John Lennon and Yoko Ono - Double Fantasy), born in Shinjuku, Tokyo, Japan
Alice Schwarzer, German journalist and feminist (EMMA), born in Wuppertal, Germany
Mike Gibson, Irish rugby union centre (69 caps Ireland, 12 British & Irish Lions, 9 Barbarians), born in Belfast, Northern Ireland

Pedro Rocha, Uruguayan soccer midfielder and manager (52 caps;
